content.assessment: Investing in comprehensive-school quality addresses a documented driver of inequality: attendance at high-poverty, under-resourced schools is associated with lower reading scores and lower secondary completion likelihood, and this operates alongside and independently of gimnázium track segregation. This compensatory approach could reduce distributional harm for students who remain in comprehensive schools regardless of whether selective tracks continue, directly benefiting the most vulnerable groups (rural, Roma, disabled students) documented as facing the highest dropout risk and lowest basic-skills attainment. However, it does not alter the sorting mechanism itself and may leave the structural segregation channel—linked to Hungary's exceptionally strong socioeconomic achievement gradient—intact.
opportunities: If effectively targeted, quality investment could narrow the achievement gap for disadvantaged and Roma students who disproportionately attend high-poverty comprehensive schools
Compensatory investment could function as a complement to, rather than substitute for, future structural reform
strengths: Directly targets documented drivers of poor outcomes for disadvantaged students: high-poverty school attendance is linked to lower reading scores and completion rates
Could plausibly improve outcomes for the most vulnerable groups (Roma, rural, disabled) whose dropout rates and basic-skills attainment are documented as severely below comparators
Does not require resolving the politically fraught structural question, potentially allowing equity gains to proceed independently of track-reform disputes
threats: Quality investment alone may not reduce demand for selective tracks if track access also serves status-signaling and family-strategy functions unrelated to actual school quality

Persistent segregation dynamics (ethnic/Roma concentration, socioeconomic sorting) may continue to concentrate the most disadvantaged students in comprehensive schools even after quality gains, limiting mobility benefits
weaknesses: Does not address the tracking mechanism itself, which is independently linked to inequality beyond quality differentials
Leaves the school-type segregation channel, identified as a major structural driver of Hungary's exceptionally strong SES achievement gradient, unaddressed
